era_context: The early 12th century CE is marked by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant, the height of the Seljuk Empire under Muhammad I Tapar, the Song Dynasty's dominance in China, and the Norman kingdoms' expansion in Europe. This is an era of religious militancy, military competition, and significant cultural and intellectual development across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major monastic centres
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary records)
The Americas (no written records in European sources)

events.title: Battle of Sarmada: Crusaders defeat Seljuks
events.description: Crusader forces under Baldwin I of Jerusalem defeated a Seljuk army at Sarmada in northern Syria. This victory was part of the broader Crusader campaign to consolidate control over the Levant and to expand their territorial holdings. The battle demonstrated the military effectiveness of Crusader cavalry tactics against Seljuk forces.

sources.name: Crusader chronicles (William of Tyre, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Sarmada is attested in Crusader sources, but details are sparse and the exact date is uncertain. The date 1117 is traditional; some sources place it in 1118 or 1119.
cross_references: Part of the broader Crusader military campaigns in the Levant
Reflects the military competition between Crusaders and Seljuks in the early 12th century

events.title: Henry V invades Italy; conflict with Pope
events.description: Henry V, King of Germany and future Holy Roman Emperor, invaded Italy to assert imperial authority and to challenge papal power. His invasion led to military conflict with papal forces and with Italian city-states. This campaign was part of the broader Investiture Controversy, in which secular and ecclesiastical authorities competed for control over the appointment of bishops.
key_figures: Henry V (King of Germany, future Holy Roman Emperor)
events.certainty_note: Henry V's Italian campaigns are attested in contemporary sources, but the exact chronology and details of his 1117 invasion are somewhat uncertain. The date 1117 is conventional; some sources place his major Italian campaigns in 1116 or 1118.
cross_references: Led to continued conflict between Henry V and the papacy, eventually resolved by the Concordat of Worms (1122)

events.title: Abelard teaching at Cathedral School of Notre-Dame
events.description: Peter Abelard was teaching at the Cathedral School of Notre-Dame in Paris, where he was gaining fame for his innovative dialectical method in theology and philosophy. His teaching was attracting large numbers of students and beginning to attract criticism from conservative ecclesiastics. This period marked the height of Abelard's intellectual influence before his condemnation.
events.certainty_note: Abelard's teaching at Notre-Dame is attested in his own writings and contemporary accounts. The exact chronology of his teaching career is somewhat uncertain; 1117 is a conventional date for his prominence.
cross_references: Part of the intellectual ferment of 12th-century Paris
Led to his condemnation at the Council of Soissons (1119)

events.title: Seljuk Sultan Muhammad I Tapar consolidates power
events.description: The Seljuk Sultan Muhammad I Tapar (r. 1105–1118) was consolidating his power and expanding Seljuk territorial control. His reign was marked by military campaigns against rival powers, administrative reforms, and cultural patronage. Muhammad I Tapar was one of the most successful Seljuk sultans and brought the empire to its territorial height.
events.certainty_note: Muhammad I Tapar's reign and his consolidation of Seljuk power are well-attested in Islamic historical sources. The date 1117 falls within his reign (1105–1118).
cross_references: Part of the Seljuk Empire's rise and consolidation
Led to the empire's territorial height before fragmentation after 1118

events.title: Song Dynasty cultural patronage under Huizong
events.description: The Huizong Emperor of the Northern Song Dynasty (r. 1100–1125) was actively patronising the arts, particularly painting, calligraphy, and ceramics. His reign was marked by significant cultural and artistic achievements, including the establishment of the Imperial Painting Academy. However, his reign was also marked by political instability and military vulnerability to the Jurchen Jin Dynasty.
key_figures: Artists and scholars of the Song court

sources.name: Song official histories and court records
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's cultural patronage is well-attested in Song historical records and surviving artworks. The date 1117 falls within his reign (1100–1125).
cross_references: Part of the Song Dynasty's cultural flourishing
Reflects the broader intellectual and artistic development of 12th-century China

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Sarmada's exact date and significance are disputed; some sources place it in 1118 or 1119, and its strategic importance may have been overstated in later Crusader chronicles. Henry V's Italian campaigns are sometimes portrayed as a unified military strategy, but modern scholarship suggests they were more reactive and fragmented, responding to specific political circumstances. Abelard's teaching career is often portrayed as a linear rise to fame, but the chronology is uncertain and his influence may have been more limited than later accounts suggest. Muhammad I Tapar's reign is sometimes characterised as the Seljuk Empire's 'height,' but this is somewhat subjective; the empire remained powerful after his death, though it did fragment into regional sultanates.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1117 is moderate for Western Europe and the Levant, where ecclesiastical and Crusader sources are available. East Asian sources (China) rely on later compilations but are based on contemporary records. Islamic sources for the Seljuk Empire are less abundant in European archives but are available in Islamic historical traditions. The surviving sources reflect the perspectives of literate elites—clergy, nobility, court officials, and intellectuals. The experiences of peasants, merchants, women (except those of high status), and non-elite populations are largely absent. The dating of several events (the Battle of Sarmada, Henry V's Italian campaigns) is uncertain, reflecting gaps in contemporary documentation. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Sarmada was part of the broader Crusader military campaigns in the Levant
Henry V's Italian campaigns and conflict with the papacy led to the negotiation and conclusion of the Concordat of Worms
Abelard's teaching and theological positions led to his condemnation at the Council of Soissons in 1119
Muhammad I Tapar's consolidation of power in 1117 was followed by his death in 1118 and the fragmentation of the Seljuk Empire
Huizong's cultural patronage and political instability led to the Jin conquest of northern China in 1127
